next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 17

By nine in the morning the Haviland Innovation Atrium smelled of lemon polish, espresso, and pastry that had been thawed somewhere else. Sun through the glass walls laid a hard white shine across the concrete floor and made the donor banners look more expensive than cloth had any right to look. The room had been built to flatter engineering. Every cord was hidden. Every table edge matched. Even the fingerprints disappeared.

Leela stood behind a retractable wall near the catering entrance with a paper folder tucked under her arm and her laptop open on a high-top table that was supposed to hold sparkling water. Her right wrist ached inside the brace. The folder held twelve printed packets, because Omar had insisted that if the wireless failed or somebody decided to be brave in theory and useless in practice, paper still counted as technology. Her phone showed three threads open at once: Omar, June, and Nina Solis from the graduate workers union, who had the encrypted archive and instructions to send it wider if all of them lost their nerve at the same time.

June arrived first, coming through the service door with a paper cup of coffee she did not seem to want. Her blunt-cut hair had been hacked shorter again, the line at one side a little crooked. She stopped long enough to flatten the cup between her hands and drop it into the trash before she said, "How many backup plans are we on now."

"Three send paths, two physical packets, and Nina if both of those fail," Leela said. "Four, if you count the timed link."

"That one doesn't count. Timed links are for peop...
open_hooks_to_carry: Slow the book down enough for aftermath to feel like an event in itself.
Use winter material detail to externalize emotional depletion without reducing the chapter to mood alone.
prev_tail_excerpt:
...blood fell from her mouth before she could turn away and stippled the words further review. Toner feathered outward in a tiny gray bruise.

That, more than the meeting, made something settle. Not calm. Not clarity generous enough to deserve a clean noun. Just the end of one particular stupidity. She had spent months believing there was still a procedural version of rescue available to her if she phrased things well enough, revised fast enough, stayed useful enough, kept the ugliest evidence in reserve long enough to bargain with. She could see the shape of that belief now because it was right there on the counter with her blood on it: a packet, a recommendation, a promise of later. Mohit's favorite terrain. Paper first, harm after, sympathy all the way through.

She bit the cheek again. Harder this time. The fresh pain flashed white behind her eyes. When she spat, the sink caught a red string and thinned it to nothing.

No room in Keating was going to give her back a future because she asked correctly. No follow-up memo was going to rescue the part of the story he had already taught the building to tell about her. The old arrangement had not failed. It had worked exactly as designed.

Her phone buzzed a third time and went still.

Leela rinsed once more, folded the damp packet shut, and held it under her arm until the paper softened against her sweater. She stayed there facing the mirror while the torn place in her cheek kept filling and emptying, filling and emptying, and the fluorescent lights hummed over the sinks like a machine too small to care what it was preserving.
state_deltas_required: Leela must decide whether to leave quietly, keep bargaining for a diminished academic future, or finally expose the full archive.
Leela encrypts and names the full evidence archive `holdout_set` and gives it to the union lawyer, choosing a path that may further damage her own prospects.
